originalTitle: Djibouti's president wins unprecedented sixth term with 97.8% of vote
title: Big News from Djibouti! A Long-Time Leader Wins Again!
content:
Hello, Amazing Friends Across Africa!

Hello, amazing friends! It's your super-duper kid news reporter from our incredible continent of Africa! Get ready to hear some big news that's happening in one of our special countries. Today, we're going to fly all the way to a place called Djibouti! Can you say Djibouti? It sounds like "Jee-BOO-tee"!

Meet President Guelleh!

Every country has a leader. This leader helps make important decisions for everyone living there. They are like the head of a very, very big family – the country family! In many countries, this leader is called a President. The big news is about the President of Djibouti. His name is Mr. Ismail Omar Guelleh. That's a fun name to say, isn't it? He is 78 years old. That's older than your grandpa or grandma, maybe! He has been the President of Djibouti for a very, very long time. For 27 years! That's longer than you've been alive, and even longer than your older brother or sister! Imagine doing the same big job for such a long, long time!

Where is Djibouti?

Djibouti is a small but super cool country in East Africa. It's right by the big, blue ocean! It's a sunny place with beautiful beaches and even some volcanoes! Imagine that! It's a land of sunshine and adventure. Did you know? Djibouti is smaller than some cities you might know! But it has a very big heart and lots of amazing things to see.

Guess What? He Won Again!

Guess what happened recently? The grown-ups in Djibouti just had a special day called an "election." An election is when grown-ups get to choose who they want to be their leader. It's like choosing the captain of your team, but for the whole country! They go to special places and mark a paper to say who they want to be President. It's a very important way for people to choose their leaders. And guess what happened? President Guelleh won the election again! Lots and lots of people chose him. Almost everyone who voted, about 97.8% of them, chose him to be their President again. That's nearly all the votes! This means he will be the President for another time. This will be his sixth time being President! Wow! That's a lot of times to be chosen as a leader.

A Very Long Time!

When a President is chosen, they usually lead for a few years. That time is called a "term." So, President Guelleh has now been chosen for his sixth term! It means he will be President for even more years. He has already led for 27 years, and now he will lead for even longer! That's a very long time to be the leader of a country. He has seen many changes and helped his country grow over these years.

Did You Know? Fun Facts About Djibouti!

Djibouti is full of wonders! Did you know Djibouti is home to Lake Assal? It's one of the saltiest lakes in the world! It's so salty that you could float really easily there, just like in the Dead Sea! It's also the lowest point in all of Africa! So cool! People in Djibouti speak two main languages: French and Arabic. And just like many people around Africa, they love coffee! Did you know? Djibouti is also an important place for ships because it has a big port on the ocean. Many ships stop there on their way to other places in the world.

What About Other People Who Wanted To Be President?

Now, there's a little bit more to this story. In some elections, other people also want to be president. They are called "candidates." This time, some of the other grown-ups who wanted to be president decided *not* to join the election. They chose not to put their names forward. So, there weren't many other choices for people to pick from on the special voting paper. This is one reason why President Guelleh got so many votes from the people who did go to choose.

Why is This Important?

So, President Guelleh will continue to lead Djibouti for another term. He will keep working hard to help make sure things are good for all the children and families in Djibouti. He'll work on important things like building more schools so kids can learn, making sure roads are good for cars and buses, and keeping everyone safe and happy. Being a president is a very big and important job!

Keep Shining, Africa!

It's amazing to learn about different countries and their leaders, isn't it? We send a big wave to all our friends in Djibouti! They are a special part of our big African family. We hope President Guelleh continues to work for a bright future for all the people in Djibouti. Keep shining bright, Africa! See you next time for more exciting news from our amazing continent!
summary: Ismail Omar Guelleh, the 78-year-old President of Djibouti, has won his sixth term as leader, extending his 27-year rule, after an election where many other candidates chose not to participate.
whyMatters: This news is important because President Guelleh will keep leading Djibouti, helping make big decisions for the country, like building schools and keeping everyone safe, just as he has done for a very long time.

title: Nearly 400 sentenced in Nigeria for links to militant Islamists
content: Judges handed down sentences ranging from five years to life imprisonment following mass trials.

originalTitle: UK shelves Chagos deal after Trump opposition
title: Islands, Friends, and a Pause Button!
content:
Hello, amazing friends! It's your kid-friendly news reporter here, bringing you a super interesting story from far, far away, across the big blue ocean. It's a story about some beautiful islands, a promise, and why sometimes, even big countries have to press a 'pause' button on their plans.

A Story About Islands Far Away

Imagine a group of tiny, green islands sparkling in a huge, blue ocean. These islands are called the Chagos Islands. They are very, very far from us, in the middle of the big Indian Ocean. These islands are so pretty, with white sandy beaches and clear water where colourful fish swim. A long, long time ago, people lived on these islands. They were happy and called themselves the Chagossians. They fished, they played, and they loved their island homes very much. But something sad happened. Many years ago, the people living on the Chagos Islands had to leave. They were asked to move away from their homes. This made many of them very sad, and they still dream of going back one day. Did you know? The Indian Ocean is the third-largest ocean in the world! It touches Africa, Asia, and Australia, and has many beautiful islands, just like the Chagos Islands.

Who Owns the Islands?

For a very long time, a country called the United Kingdom, or the UK for short, was in charge of these islands. The UK is a country far away across the sea. The UK allowed another very big country, the United States of America (which we sometimes call America), to build a very important place on one of the Chagos Islands. This special place is a military base, on an island called Diego Garcia. Think of a military base like a super important station where aeroplanes can land and take off, and boats can stop. It helps keep the world safe, and it's very important for America's helpers. Now, there's another lovely island country not too far from the Chagos Islands, called Mauritius. Mauritius is an African country, and it says that the Chagos Islands should really belong to them. They say these islands were always part of their family of islands, even though the UK was looking after them for a while. Did you know? Mauritius is famous for its dodo bird, which sadly doesn't exist anymore! But the country is still full of amazing plants and animals.

The Big Promise (The Deal)

For some time, the UK and Mauritius were talking. They were trying to make a big promise, or an 'agreement' – we can call it a 'deal'. This deal was about the UK possibly giving the Chagos Islands back to Mauritius. This would have been very exciting for many people, especially the Chagossians who were hoping it would mean they could finally go back to their old homes. Imagine how happy they would be to see their island homes again! The UK government, which is like the big boss people who run the country, was thinking hard about this. They knew it was a big decision that would make many people happy, but it also had many things to think about. Did you know? An agreement between countries is like a promise between friends. Both sides agree to do something together!

A Friend Says "Hold On!"

This is where our story gets a little bit tricky. Remember America, the country with the important base on Diego Garcia? Well, the person who used to be the leader of America, Mr. Trump, had some worries. Mr. Trump was worried that if the Chagos Islands changed hands and went back to Mauritius, it might cause problems for their very important military base. He thought it might make the base less safe or harder to use. So, Mr. Trump said, "Hold on! I don't think this is a good idea right now." This is what we call 'opposition' – when someone doesn't agree with a plan and wants it to stop or change. Because America uses the base a lot, the UK had to listen to their friend's worries. It's like when your friend asks you to play a different game because they don't like the first one you picked.

Time Ran Out

The UK government officials, those big boss people, kept talking and thinking. They wanted to figure out a way to make everyone happy: Mauritius, the Chagossians, and their friend America. But guess what? They ran out of time! Sometimes, even big countries have many things to do, and time just flies by. They couldn't finish their promise, or the 'deal', about the Chagos Islands before time was up. When we say the UK 'shelved' the deal, it's like putting a book back on a shelf. You're not throwing the book away, you're just putting it aside for a little while. You can always pick it up again later when you have more time. So, the UK had to put the plan on hold. It doesn't mean they forgot about it, or that they won't do it ever. It just means they couldn't do it *right now*.

What Happens Next?

The good news is that the UK officials said they are not giving up on the Chagos Islands story. They said they are not 'abandoning' the agreement. This means they still want to talk about it and maybe make a promise again in the future. It's like when you have a fun plan with your friends, but it starts to rain. You don't say, "We'll never play again!" You say, "Let's try again tomorrow!" So, the story of the Chagos Islands and who they belong to is not over. It's just taking a little break. We hope that one day, everyone can be happy and the Chagossians can finally go home if they wish.

Why This Story Matters to Us

This story is important because it teaches us about fairness, promises, and how countries try to work things out. It shows us that even big countries can have problems to solve and friends to listen to. It also reminds us of the beautiful islands and people in our very own African region, like Mauritius and the Chagossians, and how their stories are important to the whole world. So, even though the promise about the Chagos Islands is on a pause for now, we know that people are still thinking about it. And maybe, one day soon, the play button will be pressed again! Stay tuned for more news from your kid-friendly reporter!
